metadata.summary: Systematic framework for generating consistent organic traffic through content: define pillars, research keywords, create content, distribute, measure, iterate. Core rule: spend 50% of total content production time on distribution.

content.title: Content Marketing
content.summary: A systematic framework for generating consistent organic traffic through content: define pillars, research keywords, create content, distribute, measure, iterate. The core rule: spend 50% of total content production time on distribution — publishing without distribution is the primary reason content marketing fails to produce traffic.

why.p: Content marketing is the only paid-acquisition alternative that compounds over time: a well-ranked blog post generates traffic for years at zero marginal cost. Most content marketing fails not from poor writing but from random publishing (no pillar strategy), writing for the wrong audience, or treating SEO as an afterthought. A structured pillar + keyword intent + distribution checklist approach cuts time-to-ranking by removing guesswork.

list.item: Generating keyword research lists and content briefs for a defined topic cluster
Drafting long-form blog posts from a structured brief (keyword, intent, outline, competitors)
Building a 3-month content calendar with topics mapped to funnel stages
Repurposing a long-form piece into 5-7 derivative formats (tweet thread, newsletter, LinkedIn, carousel)
Writing distribution checklists and outreach messages for newly published content
Defining content pillars — requires ICP interviews and product positioning work, not synthesis
Content that depends on original data, proprietary research, or lived experience
Publishing directly to any CMS without human review
Predicting SEO performance — agents cannot query live search engine data
Replacing a content strategist's judgment on topic prioritization for a specific brand

rule.statement: Define 3-5 content pillars before any keyword research or content creation; each pillar must map to a specific ICP pain point.
rule.rationale: Random content production confuses topical authority signals; pillars enable the algorithm to classify the site as an authority on specific topics, not a generalist.

example.title: Email marketing tool content pillars
example.code: 1. Email marketing tactics (core product alignment) 2. Newsletter growth (adjacent pain point) 3. Email copywriting (skill needed by ICP) Content mix: 60% SEO-focused, 30% thought leadership, 10% product updates Publishing cadence: 2 posts/week

list.item: Awareness: blog posts, social content, podcasts — attract via informational keywords
Consideration: guides, comparisons, webinars — educate on alternatives
Decision: case studies, demos, free trials — remove objections
Retention: tutorials, product updates, community — keep customers activated

rule.statement: Classify every keyword by intent before assigning a content type; wrong content type for the intent reduces conversion even with good rankings.
rule.rationale: A transactional keyword ("notion pricing") requires a product page, not a blog post. Mismatched intent wastes creation effort.

list.item: Informational ("how to manage remote teams"): blog post or guide
Commercial ("best project management tools"): comparison or listicle
Transactional ("notion pricing"): product page or pricing page
Navigational ("slack login"): homepage, do not target with content

rule.statement: Prioritize high-volume + low-difficulty keywords first; skip low-volume + high-difficulty combinations entirely.
rule.rationale: High-difficulty keywords require domain authority that takes 12-18 months to build; starting with low-difficulty wins generates traffic and builds authority in parallel.

list.item: High volume + low difficulty: quick wins — do first
High volume + high difficulty: long-term plays — plan for months 6-12
Low volume + low difficulty: easy wins — fill gaps
Low volume + high difficulty: skip entirely

section.title: Content Brief Requirements

rule.statement: Every piece of content requires a brief with: primary keyword, search intent, target audience, unique angle, and 2 competitor URLs to beat.
rule.rationale: Agent output quality is directly proportional to brief specificity; vague briefs produce commodity content. Competitor URLs improve output quality significantly.

section.reference: Full content brief template with all required fields

rule.statement: Structure every blog post as: keyword-rich headline → hook → definition → why it matters → step-by-step → examples → mistakes → tools → conclusion + CTA.
rule.rationale: This structure matches the top-ranked content pattern for informational queries across most niches; deviating requires a clear differentiation reason.

rule.statement: Every published post must be better than the top 3 results and contain at least one piece of original insight, data, or example not present in competitors.
rule.rationale: Generic aggregation content does not rank or convert. E-E-A-T (experience, expertise, authoritativeness, trustworthiness) requires original contribution.

section.title: Distribution Rule

rule.statement: Spend 50% of total content production time on distribution; publishing without distribution is the primary reason content marketing fails to produce traffic.
rule.rationale: The "if you build it they will come" assumption fails in competitive niches. Every piece needs at least 5-7 distribution touchpoints in the first week.

section.reference: Day-1/Week-1/ongoing distribution steps per published piece

list.item: Owned: email newsletter (day 1), blog, social profiles
Earned: guest posts, podcast appearances, PR mentions, community shares
Paid amplification: promoted posts, content discovery (Outbrain/Taboola), retargeting

section.title: Content ROI Formula
section.p: Content ROI = (Revenue from Content - Content Cost) / Content Cost x 100%

example.title: Concrete ROI calculation example
example.code: Blog drives 1,000 visits/month 2% convert to trial, 10% trial-to-paid conversion 20 customers x $50/month = $1,000 MRR Content cost: $500/month (writer + tools) ROI = ($1,000 - $500) / $500 = 100%

list.item: Traffic: Google Analytics (reach)
Time on page + scroll depth: engagement quality
Keyword rankings: Google Search Console
Backlinks: Ahrefs or Moz (domain authority signal)
Conversions: GA + product analytics (actual business impact)

antipattern.description: Publishing without a distribution plan — "if you build it they will come" approach.
antipattern.reason: Content discovery does not happen organically in competitive niches; active distribution is required for the first 12 months.

antipattern.description: Giving up before 12 months of consistent publishing.
antipattern.reason: SEO compounds over 6-12 months; early months produce minimal traffic regardless of content quality. Quitting before compounding begins wastes all prior investment.

antipattern.description: Quantity over quality — publishing 5+ thin posts per week.
antipattern.reason: Low-quality content hurts domain authority and brand credibility; fewer better posts outperform in rankings and conversion.

antipattern.description: Ignoring internal linking between related posts.
antipattern.reason: Internal links distribute page authority and signal topical clusters to search engines; absence reduces ranking potential of all posts.

template.purpose: Full content brief with keyword, intent, outline, competitors, CTA
Monthly content calendar table with weekly structure
Day-1/Week-1/ongoing distribution steps per new piece
